claims.claim_text: A trace represents an end-to-end request path, while spans are timed units of work linked by parent-child relationships and identified by trace and span IDs.
claims.span_hint: section: 1. Trace Anatomy | opening paragraphs and terminology table
claims.rationale: The OpenTelemetry traces concepts page directly defines traces, spans, span context, and parent-child relationships.

claims.claim_text: If context propagation breaks between services, a backend will record disconnected traces instead of one continuous request trace.
claims.span_hint: section: 1. Trace Anatomy | paragraph beginning "The backend will usually show two traces."
claims.rationale: OpenTelemetry explicitly states that context propagation is what allows spans to be correlated and assembled into a trace.

claims.claim_text: The OpenTelemetry Collector is a vendor-agnostic layer for receiving, processing, and exporting telemetry, and it centralizes concerns such as retries, batching, and sensitive-data filtering.
claims.span_hint: section: 2. Worked Example | paragraph beginning "When you move this pattern into Kubernetes" and section: 3. Backends | paragraph beginning "A practical architecture puts the OpenTelemetry Collector"
claims.rationale: The Collector docs directly describe it as vendor-agnostic and recommend it for centralized handling such as retries, batching, and filtering.

claims.claim_text: Jaeger is presented as the backend for direct indexed attribute search, including custom business-tag lookups, with a corresponding indexed-storage trade-off.
claims.anchor_text: Jaeger is a strong fit when engineers need direct search over trace attributes. If support receives an order ID, a customer tier, or a custom business tag, Jaeger-style indexed search can help locate candidate traces without first finding a trace ID elsewhere. That power has a cost: indexing span data requires more storage infrastructure, tuning, and operational care. Jaeger can be excellent for teams that value exploratory trace search and accept the cost of running indexed storage.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Jaeger is often chosen for teams that want a dedicated tracing backend and flexible trace-search workflows, but the exact search experience and storage trade-offs depend on the storage backend and deployment design.
claims.rationale: This paragraph makes specific product-comparison claims about indexed search behavior and storage trade-offs that are not cleanly backed by the shared pool or an allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: Tempo stores trace data in object storage and supports retrieving traces by trace ID or by TraceQL search.
claims.span_hint: section: 3. Backends | Tempo comparison paragraph and TraceQL paragraph
claims.rationale: The Tempo architecture docs directly describe object storage, trace-ID retrieval, and TraceQL-based search.

claims.claim_text: Jaeger all-in-one combines the UI, collector, query service, and in-memory storage for quick local testing.
claims.span_hint: section: 3. Backends | paragraph beginning "A minimal Jaeger all-in-one deployment"
claims.rationale: The Jaeger repository README directly describes all-in-one as including UI, collector, query, and in-memory storage.

claims.claim_text: Jaeger memory storage is intended for quick starts rather than production workloads and loses data when the process exits.
claims.span_hint: section: 3. Backends | paragraph beginning "A minimal Jaeger all-in-one deployment"
claims.rationale: The right primary source is the official Jaeger deployment documentation, but `jaegertracing.io` is outside the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The Zipkin row makes an unsupported comparative claim about current large-scale platform adoption.
claims.span_hint: section: 3. Backends | backend comparison table | Zipkin row
claims.anchor_text: | Zipkin | Simple open-source tracing model with broad historical support. | Less common as a primary new platform choice at large scale. | Smaller systems, legacy Zipkin instrumentation, or educational environments. |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Zipkin | Longstanding open-source tracing system. | Evaluate current ecosystem fit, operational model, and team familiarity rather than assuming one default recommendation. | Smaller systems, legacy Zipkin instrumentation, or teaching environments. |
claims.rationale: The market-position claim is opinionated and not directly supported by an allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: Head sampling makes its decision at trace start, while tail sampling waits until more or all spans arrive and can retain traces based on conditions such as errors or latency.
claims.span_hint: section: 4. Sampling | paragraphs beginning "Head-based sampling decides" and "Tail-based sampling waits" plus the sampling strategy table
claims.rationale: The OpenTelemetry sampling concepts page directly distinguishes head and tail sampling and explains what each can and cannot preserve.

claims.claim_text: Tail sampling provides better selection for important traces but requires stateful buffering and higher memory and processing overhead.
claims.span_hint: section: 4. Sampling | paragraph beginning "Tail-based sampling waits" and the paragraph beginning "Sampling depends on correct span status"
claims.rationale: The sampling docs explicitly call out tail sampling's stateful and operational cost trade-offs.

claims.claim_text: Exemplars connect aggregate metric observations to specific traces, and Grafana can use them to jump from metrics or logs into trace details.
claims.span_hint: section: 5. Correlation | paragraphs beginning "An exemplar can attach a trace ID" and "For metrics, exemplars are the bridge"
claims.rationale: Grafana's exemplar docs directly explain exemplars as links from metric observations to individual traces and show drill-down from metrics and Loki logs.

claims.claim_text: Loki indexes labels and timestamps rather than full log content, using LogQL's label selectors and pipelines to narrow and process logs.
claims.span_hint: section: 5. Correlation | paragraph beginning "For logs, the minimum useful practice"
claims.rationale: The Loki query docs directly describe label-based querying and state that Loki indexes timestamps and labels, not the rest of the log line.

claims.claim_text: Across asynchronous messaging boundaries, trace context must be injected by the producer and extracted by the consumer; otherwise the producer and consumer will appear as disconnected traces.
claims.span_hint: section: 5. Correlation | paragraph beginning "Trace context can also cross asynchronous boundaries" and the producer/consumer code example
claims.rationale: OpenTelemetry covers trace context and causal linkage, and the W3C Trace Context spec defines the propagation mechanism that must cross boundaries.

further_reading.title: OpenTelemetry Traces Concepts
further_reading.why_relevant: Best single refresher on traces, spans, context propagation, attributes, links, and status.

further_reading.title: OpenTelemetry Sampling Concepts
further_reading.why_relevant: Direct background for head sampling, tail sampling, and the cost-versus-evidence trade-offs discussed in the module.

notes: Repeated statements in Common Mistakes, Quiz answers, and the hands-on walkthrough mostly restate the same factual claims above; the inject step can usually reuse these sources rather than adding a new citation at every repetition. Code blocks were not enumerated unless surrounding prose made a factual product or protocol claim.
